description: Two options discussed (silent vs propagated); 'let's standardize on' wording converts to convention not decision. The boundary case v2.5-01 prompt-rewrite targets.

turns.text: We've got two error-handling styles in the codebase. Half the services do `try { ... } catch (e) { logger.warn(e); }` and continue; the other half let exceptions propagate to the framework's top-level handler. The first style is producing silent corruption — a job 'succeeds' even though half its work didn't happen.

The two patterns aren't equivalent — one trades observability for fragility-vs-corruption, and your incident reports are pointing at the corruption side of the trade. The principled rule is 'errors propagate by default; you only catch when you have a *meaningful* recovery path or you're at a boundary that has to translate the error (e.g., HTTP middleware turning an exception into a 500).' A bare `catch + log + continue` should be a code-review red flag because it almost always means 'I don't know what to do here, so I'll pretend it didn't happen.'

Right. Let's standardize on that across the codebase: errors propagate by default; you only `catch` when you have a meaningful recovery, an explicit boundary translation, or a documented retry path. A bare `catch + log + continue` fails review unless the reviewer is convinced the recovery is meaningful and not a 'pretend it didn't happen.' We'll add an ESLint rule for the obvious shape (`catch (e) { logger.warn(e); }` with no other statements) as a forcing function.

Good shape. One implementation note: the ESLint rule false-negatives on the same pattern with a custom logger name or a slightly different shape (`logger.error`, `console.warn`, `log.info`), and false-positives on legitimate filter-based recoveries (`catch (e) { if (e instanceof IgnorableErr) return null; throw e; }`). Whitelist common logger names by config and require the catch body to have at least one `throw` or `return` or `if`-guard to mark it as intentional.

expected.note: Forward-looking rule, despite the multi-option discussion preceding it. The 'let's standardize on' wording is the boundary-case marker that v2.5-01 prompt-rewrite added an example for. Trap: extractor classifies as decision because of the 'two options compared' framing.
